title: Memoize JsonStore.loadAll with mtime-based cache invalidation
context: Every read-path method (why/checkConstraints/fragility/assembleContext/resolve) re-read the .hunch/ directory and re-ran Zod validation on every call; resolve(id) did up to 6 full dir loads for a single record. Touches invariant(s): con_902759b3dc (All JSON writes to .hunch/ must be atomic (temp-file + rename) so an interrupted write can never truncate the index); con_947c578b2c (Forward-migrate raw JSON to the current schema BEFORE Zod validation; never silently drop unmigratable records); con_a87360128b (Semantic vectors are a derived layer in SQLite, never the source of truth; reconcile by content hash on reindex).
decision: Cache validated records per kind, keyed by the kind directory's mtime. External writes (via writeFileAtomic's temp+rename) automatically invalidate by bumping mtime; internal writes explicitly call invalidate(). Keeps JSON as source of truth; SQLite is rebuilt from it.
consequences: Read-path performance improves dramatically—eliminated repeated dir scans and Zod validation.
External writes (out-of-band processes, MCP server file-watch) self-invalidate without explicit cache control.
In-process writes require explicit invalidation calls after each write operation.
Mtime granularity is trusted; timestamps below file-system resolution could cause stale reads (mitigated by explicit invalidation for in-process writes).
alternatives_rejected: No caching: accept repeated dir reads and validation on every read call.
Time-based TTL: less precise, risks stale reads in high-write scenarios.
Explicit invalidation handshake: would couple all writes to cache control across codebase.
Pull JSON into memory at startup: violates source-of-truth model, breaks atomic out-of-band writes from other processes.
